coredata.dc:title: Ultrasmall organosilica nanoparticles with strong solid-state fluorescence for multifunctional applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Cairo University.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Cairo University.
coredata.dc:creator: Wan, Jianqin
Liang, Jiahao
Xian, Shiyun
Gong, Xiao
Wang, Hangxiang
coredata.dc:description:
Introduction Organosilica nanoparticles (ONs), which are a new type of photoluminescent nanomaterial (PM) with excellent biocompatibility, have caught more attention in recent years. However, their applications are significantly impeded by the complicated preparation process, poor photostability, and especially aggregation-induced quenching.
Objectives The present study was aimed to design and prepare solid-state fluorescent ONs to avoid aggregation-induced quenching effect. In addition, the uses of ONs for fingerprint detection, white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) and lysosome-targetable cellular imaging were demonstrated.
Methods Here, for the first time, we designed and prepared novel solid-state fluorescent ultrasmall ONs with orange-emitting photoluminescence via a one-step hydrothermal method.
Results The prepared solid-state fluorescent ONs could be successfully employed in fingerprint detection, WLEDs fabrication and cellular imaging. Intriguingly, the ultrasmall ONs specifically localized to lysosomes rather than other subcellular organelles across distinct cell lines, including cancer cells and noncancerous cells.
Conclusion Collectively, these data showed that the new ONs presented in this study could be ideal candidates for PMs in biological and photoelectric applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solid-state fluorescence
Ultrasmall organosilica nanoparticles
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of polymer-like coated ONs-based WLEDs
Collection and imaging of latent fingerprints
Photostability of ONs as a LysoTracker probe
Morphology and structure of polymer coating structured ONs
Application of ONs powder in fingerprint detection
Application of ONs powder in WLED devices
ONs serve as a new LysoTracker

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Research Project of Jinan Microecological Biomedicine Shandong Laboratory (JNL-2022010B), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 82073296 , 81773193 , and 21774098 ), and Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LR19H160002 ). This study is also financially supported by the Opening Project of State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering of China (Sichuan University) (Grant No. sklpme2019-4-26).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the polymer coating structured ultrasmall ONs formation and the possible formation mechanism.

(a) Typical TEM image of ultrasmall ONs with the polymer coating. (b) The average size of ONs. (c) FT-IR spectrum of the polymer coating structured ONs powder. (d) XRD pattern of the polymer coating structured ONs.

XPS spectra of the polymer coating structured ONs. (a) Full scan of polymer coating structured ONs and XPS peak spectrum with high-resolution of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) O 1s, (e) Si 2p, and (f) Zn 2p.

(a) UV–vis spectra of the ONs in aqueous solution (insets: the photos of the ONs in aqueous solutions under natural light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right)). (b) Excitation-independent spectra of the ONs in aqueous solutions. (c) Fluorescence decay curves of the ONs in aqueous solutions. (d) UV–vis spectra of the ONs powder (insets: the photos of the ONs powder under natural light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right)) (e) Excitation-independent spectra of the ONs powder. (f) Fluorescence decay curves of the ONs powder.
Representative fluorescent images (left) and magnified features (right) of latent fingerprint recognition based on polymer coating structured ONs powder on aluminum foil.

Fluorescent images of latent fingerprint recognition on (a) glass, (b) plastic, (c) weighting paper and (d) coin based on the ONs powder under natural light (left) and UV light (middle and right). Details in the right panels were from the main panels under UV irradiation, which show the fingerprint of whorl and termination.

Endo/lysosome-targeting performance of ONs and the endocytic pathway in SUN-449 cells. Representative CLSM images of SUN-449 cells costained with ONs (25 µg/mL) and organelle trackers: a) LysoTracker Red DND-99 (100 nM, 30 min) for lysosomes, b) MitoTracker Red CMXRos (200 nM, 50 min) for mitochondria, ER-Tracker Red (1 µM, 50 min) for endoplasmic reticulum, and Hoechst 33,324 (10 µg/mL, 20 min) for nuclei. c) The internalization efficiency of ONs after 4 h incubation at 37 °C or 4 °C analyzed with flow cytometry. d) Analysis of ONs uptake in the presence or absence of endocytosis inhibitors (cytochalasin D, filipin and chlorpromazine). The data was presented as the mean ± SD (n = 3).

(a) Photostability of ONs (with an increasing number of scans (1–30 scan)) in comparison with that of LysoTracker Red DND-99. (b) Fluorescence intensity decay of ONs and LysoTracker Red DND-99 derived from a. F0 is the initial fluorescence intensity. F represents the fluorescence intensity after an increasing number of scans. Data was shown as the mean ± SD (n = 4).

head.ce:title: Ultrasmall organosilica nanoparticles with strong solid-state fluorescence for multifunctional applications

Wang).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: NHC Key Laboratory of Combined Multi-Organ Transplantation
Research Center for Diagnosis and Treatment of Hepatobiliary Diseases

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Organosilica nanoparticles (ONs), which are a new type of photoluminescent nanomaterial (PM) with excellent biocompatibility, have caught more attention in recent years. However, their applications are significantly impeded by the complicated preparation process, poor photostability, and especially aggregation-induced quenching.

The present study was aimed to design and prepare solid-state fluorescent ONs to avoid aggregation-induced quenching effect. In addition, the uses of ONs for fingerprint detection, white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) and lysosome-targetable cellular imaging were demonstrated.
Here, for the first time, we designed and prepared novel solid-state fluorescent ultrasmall ONs with orange-emitting photoluminescence via a one-step hydrothermal method.

The prepared solid-state fluorescent ONs could be successfully employed in fingerprint detection, WLEDs fabrication and cellular imaging. Intriguingly, the ultrasmall ONs specifically localized to lysosomes rather than other subcellular organelles across distinct cell lines, including cancer cells and noncancerous cells.
Collectively, these data showed that the new ONs presented in this study could be ideal candidates for PMs in biological and photoelectric applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanomaterials with high quantum yields and tunable fluorescence properties are in high demand and have drawn increasing attention due to their wide applications [1]. Semiconductor quantum dots have been developed significantly and seem to be ideal fluorescent materials due to the excellent fluorescence properties, but their applications are obviously limited by their toxicities. Organosilica nanoparticles (ONs), a new type of photoluminescent nanomaterial, have the characteristics of both organic and inorganic materials through the rational combination of organic and inorganic components into a single platform [2]. ONs differ from typical inorganosilica nanoparticles fabricated from tetraethoxyorthosilicate regarding structure and function since ONs usually contain interior and exterior functionalities. In addition to having outstanding thermal stability and radiation resistance compared with other polymer materials [3,4], ONs also show good insulation and biocompatibility [5,6]. Once the side chains contain organic fluorescent groups, ONs exhibit superior fluorescent properties and high stability to meet the requirements of applications in photoelectric devices and biological fields. Since Blaaderen et al. [7] covalently conjugated organic fluorophores into a colloidal organosilica matrix by an improved Stöber method for the first time, researchers have paid more attention to the preparation and application of fluorescent ONs [8–14].

Currently, most studies focus on tuning the emission color of ONs and/or improving the quantum yield of photoluminescence. Unfortunately, the resonance energy transfer phenomenon in the aggregated state occurs in fluorescent ONs, resulting in fluorescence quenching, which significantly limits their applications in many fields, such as ion detection [15–17], light-emitting diodes [18], luminescent solar concentrators [1,19–22], fingerprint detection [11,23], and biological imaging [24,25]. Therefore, inhibiting the fluorescence quenching of fluorescent ONs and maintaining their fluorescence properties in the aggregated state remains a challenge.

Rose bengal, as a typical small-molecule organic dye, is a tetraiodine-substituted heteranthracene dye with a large absorption coefficient in the visible light region. Owing to containing heavy atoms, strong spin-orbit coupling exists in rose bengal, leading to a high tendency to have intersystem crossing, which weakens the fluorescence [26]. Due to its strong intersystem crossing, the fluorescence performance of rose bengal is poor, which makes it to have few applications in the fluorescent field. Thus, to broaden the application of rose bengal, it is crucial to reduce intersystem crossing by removing heavy atoms to enhance the fluorescence.

Combing organic dye molecules into colloidal organosilica represents an efficient approach to improving the fluorescence intensity and photostability of dyes and obviously preventing their aggregation quenching. In this work, we attempted to integrate rose bengal into the ON structure to generate a solid-state fluorescent material. To this end, polymer-like coated solid-state fluorescent ultrasmall ONs were structurally designed and synthesized by a simple hydrothermal approach using rose bengal, N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl) propyl] ethylenediamine (DAMO), and zinc chloride as precursors. Characterization of the resulting nanoparticles revealed that rose bengal was successfully conjugated into ONs and that the heavy atoms of rose bengal were reduced through the hydrothermal method. Because of the polymer coating structure, the synthesized ultrasmall ONs are able to avoid the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect and maintain strong fluorescence signals in solid or solutions. Furthermore, we demonstrated several successful uses of ONs in rapid latent fingerprints detection, white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs), and fluorescence imaging of lysosomes in living cells, thereby highlighting the great potential of ONs in photoelectric and biological applications.

Rose bengal (95%), N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (DAMO, 95%) and zinc chloride (ZnCl2, ≥98%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Ethanol (EtOH, ≥99.7%) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Ba, Sr)2SiO4:Eu2+ powder and 460 nm LED chips were purchased from Looking Long Technology Co., Ltd. Epoxy resins (E51 + W93) were purchased from Kunshan Jiulimei Electronic Materials Co., Ltd. LysoTracker Red DND-99 and ER-Tracker Red (BODIPY® TR Glibenclamide) were purchased from Yeasen Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hoechst 33342 and MitoTracker Red CMXRos were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). All reagents were used without further purification.

Typically, 30 mg of rose bengal and 0.18 g of zinc chloride were dissolved in 4 mL of deionized water and stirred at room temperature for 10 min. Then, 1 mL of DAMO was slowly added into the solution and stirred for a few minutes to obtain a clear solution. Afterward, the solution was transferred into a 10 mL of Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept at 160 °C for 4 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the solution was purified by adding three volumes of EtOH and centrifuging at 3000 rpm for 3 min. When the precipitate was removed, another three volumes of EtOH were added to the solution. Much precipitate can be obtained by simply shaking and mixing. The solid-state ONs were collected by centrifuging at 8000 rpm for 3 min and finally dried in an oven at 60 °C for 3 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of polymer-like coated ONs-based WLEDs
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, ONs powders were mixed with commercial (Ba, Sr)2SiO4:Eu2+ powders with green fluorescence. Then, the mixed powders and epoxy resin glue A were stirred uniformly and integrated on 460 nm LED chips after adding epoxy glue B. Finally, the WLED device was obtained after drying at 323 K for 3 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Collection and imaging of latent fingerprints
ce:section.ce:para: For latent fingerprint imaging, the powder method was used for actual detection. First, a volunteer was requested to rub their finger on their nose or hair and then to press the finger on various substrate surfaces, including glass, weighting paper, aluminized paper, plastic and a coin. Then, polymer-like coated ONs powder was sprinkled on the surface of the latent fingerprints, and the excess powder was carefully blown away by a rubber suction bulb. Finally, fluorescence images were captured by an iPhone 11 under 365 nm UV light irradiation.

Human hepatoma 7402 and SUN-449 cells and noncancerous cell lines, including Raw 264.7 and HUVEC cells, were purchased from the cell bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). All cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin (100 units/mL), and streptomycin (100 μg/mL) in an incubator with 5% CO2 at 37 °C.

To evaluate the cytotoxicities of ONs against human hepatoma cell lines (7402 and SUN-449 cells) and noncancerous cell lines (HUVEC and Raw 264.7 cells), a CCK-8 assay was conducted. Cells were seeded at a density of 2 × 104-3 × 104 cells per well in 96-well plates and cultured overnight at 37 °C. Following 12 h incubation with serial dilutions of ONs (0.028, 0.28, 1.4, 7.0, 14.0, 28.0 µg/mL), the culture medium for each well was removed and replenished with 100 µL of fresh medium containing 10% CCK8 (v/v). Subsequently, all plates were incubated at 37 °C for 1–2 h. The absorbance was determined with a microplate reader (Multiskan FC, Thermo Scientific) at 450 nm.

SUN-449 cells were placed in 6-well plates (3 × 105 cells per well) and cultured at 37 °C overnight. Following 4 h incubation with ONs (25.0 µg/mL) at 37 °C or 4 °C, cells were harvested for flow cytometry analysis (Cytoflex, Beckman, USA). To determine the endocytic pathway of ONs, cells were pretreated with the specific endocytic inhibitor chlorpromazine (10 µg/mL), cytochalasin D (40 µM) or filipin III (5 µg/mL) for 30 min and then incubated with ONs at 37 °C for another 4 h. After incubation, cells were harvested and analyzed with flow cytometry (Cytoflex, Beckman, USA).

SUN-449, 7402 and Raw 264.7 cells were seeded in glass-bottom culture dishes (Φ 20 mm) at a density of 8 × 104 cells per well and cultured overnight. After incubation with ONs (25 µg/mL) at 37 °C for 4 h, cells were rinsed with PBS three times, followed by staining with LysoTracker Red DND-99 (100 nM, 30 min), MitoTracker Red CMXRos (200 nM, 50 min), ER-Tracker Red (1 µM, 50 min) and Hoechst 33,324 (10 µg/mL, 20 min). Upon complete washes, cells were observed on Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, (Olympus IX81-FV3000, Japan) with a 60 oil-immersion objective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photostability of ONs as a LysoTracker probe
ce:section.ce:para: The photostability of ONs was compared with that of the commercial lysosomal tracker probe LysoTracker Red DND-99 after long-term irradiation. SUN-449 cells were seeded in glass-bottom culture dishes (Φ 20 mm) at a density of 8 × 104 cells per well and cultured overnight. Subsequently, cells were contained with ONs (25 µg/mL, 3 h) and LysoTracker Red DND-99 (100 nM, 30 min). Upon complete washes, cells were scanned on CLSM (Olympus IX81-FV3000, Japan) every 15.4 s for 463 s. For ONs, excitation: 488 nm; emission collected: 500–540 nm. For LysoTracker Red DND-99, excitation: 561 nm; emission collected: 570–670 nm.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV2600, Shimadzu Co., Tokyo, Japan) using a quartz cuvette as a container with a 10 mm optical path. Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was carried out with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (QM/TM/NIR, Photon Technology International Co., USA). The PL emission was recorded at different excitation wavelengths in the range 370–650 nm. The fluorescence lifetimes were collected by a time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) spectrometer. The absolute PLQYs of polymer-like coated ONs powder were measured by a UV-NIR quantum yield spectrometer (Quantaurus-QY Plus, Hamamatsu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a JEM-2100F electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed using a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany), and JCPDS databases and Highscore software were used for XRD pattern recognizing. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained in an FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet 6700, Thermo Electron Co., USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Electron Co., USA), and Avantage software was used for XPS fitting. The electroluminescence (EL) spectra of WLEDs were measured using an HP9000 LED fast spectrum analyzer. The cellular uptake mechanism for ONs was explored with flow cytometry (Cytoflex, Beckman, USA). Cell imaging was performed on a CLSM (Olympus IX81-FV3000, Japan) with a 60 oil-immersion objective. The absorbance for the CCK-8 assay was determined with a microplate reader (Multiskan FC, Thermo Scientific) at 450 nm.

As displayed in Fig. 1 , ultrasmall ONs with polymer coating structure were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal approach. DAMO and rose bengal were used as precursors, and zinc ions were added and played a role in the formation of network structure. After purification, polymer coating ultrasmall ONs powder could be obtained by precipitation with ethanol and dry in oven. A possible mechanism of the formation of polymer coating structured ultrasmall ONs is shown in Fig. 1. During the hydrothermal reaction, Si-O-Si cages and chain structure are produced, and a Si-O-Si network structure is simultaneously formed, resulting in the polymer coating structured ultrasmall ONs formation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of polymer coating structured ONs
ce:section.ce:para: The typical TEM image as shown in Fig. 2 a reveals that the synthesized ultrasmall ONs are uniformly distributed in the structure like polymer coating. Approximately 100 ONs were used to statistically calculate the size distribution. Results shows that the ONs size is mainly from 2.4 nm to 6.4 nm, and the average size is about 4.4 nm (Fig. 2b). In order to study the structure of these ultrasmall ONs with polymer coating, the FT-IR spectrum was tested and analyzed, as shown in Fig. 2c. The FT-IR spectrum shows that there are obvious absorption peaks in 400–4000 cm−1. The vibration of the NH/OH bond appears at 3260–3500 cm−1 [27], and the stretching vibration of the CH bond occurs at the positions of 2935 cm−1 and 2881 cm−1 [28]. Furthermore, there have characteristic vibration peaks of CO (1654 cm−1) and NH (1602 cm−1) of amide bonds [27], and the CC vibration peaks (1465 cm−1 and 1340 cm−1) of rose bengal are clearly detected in the spectrum [29], indicating that rose bengal is successfully connected to ONs through covalent bonds. In addition, there are many characteristic peaks of organosilica. The stretching vibration of CN is located at the position of 1409 cm−1 [29], the peaks located at 1135 and 1097 cm−1 are the vibration of the SiOSi bond, indicating the structures of SiOSi cage and SiOSi ring/network are formed, and the peak located at 1027 cm−1 corresponds to the vibration peak of SiOSi chain structure [30–32]. The absorption peaks ascribing to the stretching and bending vibrations of SiC bonds are located at 1313 cm−1 and 779 cm−1, respectively [11,33–35], The absorption peak at 688 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of OSiO, and the absorption at 453 cm−1 is the bending vibration of SiOSi [11,33,36]. The XRD pattern of the dye-type ONs with the polymer coating is presented in Fig. 2d, which shows two broad peaks of 11° and 22°, corresponding to the polymer-like silicone network structure and amorphous silica [37,38].

Besides, XPS characterizations were used to investigate the chemical composition of the polymer coating structured ONs. According to Fig. 3 a, the XPS spectrum with full-scan of polymer coating structured ONs shows the existence of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, Zn 2p, Zn 3p, Si 2p and Cl 2p in the structure. There is no I element in the full scan XPS spectrum, indicating that the I element in rose bengal has been removed by hydrothermal reaction. However, Cl can be detected in the full scanning spectrum, which may be introduced by the raw material of ZnCl2 or the remaining Cl from rose bengal. In high-resolution XPS spectrum, C 1s is able to be fitted to five peaks at the positions of 283.5 eV, 284.6 eV, 285.4 eV, 286.4 eV, and 288 eV, respectively. These peaks correspond to CSi, CC/CC, CN, CO, and CO, respectively, which is shown in Fig. 3b [39,40]. The N 1s band is able to be divided into two peaks at the positions of 399 eV, and 400.4 eV, ascribing to CNC and CNH2 (Fig. 3c), respectively [41,42]. The O 1s band is able to be divided into three peaks at the positions of 530.3 eV, 531.3 eV and 531.6 eV, respectively, which are attributed to CO, CO and SiO, as shown in Fig. 3d [11,43,44]. The Si 2p band may be fitted to two peaks at the positions of 101.0 eV and 101.8 eV, which are attributed to SiC and SiO, as shown in Fig. 3e [45–47]. According to Fig. 3f, the Zn 2p band is able to be divided into Zn 2p 3/2 of 1021.5 eV, and Zn 2p 1/2 of 1044.7 eV, which shows the existence of +2 valence state Zn ions [48]. The FT-IR and XPS spectra show that many amino and hydroxyl groups exist in the polymer coating structured ONs, which provides the material with good water dispersibility.

The optical properties of the ultrasmall ONs in aqueous solutions and in the powder state were tested and analyzed. The UV–vis spectrum of the ONs which were dispersed in aqueous solutions exhibits strong absorption in the range of 420–560 nm, and the maximum absorption peak of the spectrum is located at 512 nm, as shown in Fig. 4 a. Unlike that dispersed in solution, the absorption peak of the ONs powder has an obvious change, as shown in Fig. 4d. The powder has strong absorption at 450–600 nm, and the maximum absorption peak of the powder is at 535 nm. The redshift of the fluorescent powder may be caused by the excimer formed during aggregation. Photos of the ONs (insets of Fig. 4a and d) in the aqueous and solid state reveal that both of them exhibit strong fluorescence, indicating that the polymer coating structure can effectively inhibit the aggregation quenching of ONs.

Under excitation wavelengths from 435 nm to 525 nm, the ONs which are dispersed in aqueous solution emit yellow-green fluorescence with a fluorescence peak of 525 nm. With increasing excitation wavelength, the fluorescence emission peak remains unchanged, indicating that the ONs which are dispersed in aqueous solution have no excitation wavelength dependence (Fig. 4b). Fluorescence lifetime of the ONs in aqueous solution was tested by a time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer, and the fluorescence attenuation curve of the material was obtained, as shown in Fig. 4c. The intensity of the ONs fluorescence decays exponentially, and the fluorescence lifetime is 3.77 ns.

Unlike the ONs in aqueous solution, once the excitation wavelength changes from 460 nm to 550 nm, the fluorescence of the ONs powder emits orange at 570 nm, showing redshifts compared with the aqueous solution (Fig. 4e). The redshift can also be attributed to the formation of the excimer during aggregation. There is also no excitation wavelength dependence in the solid state. The fluorescence lifetime of the polymer coating structured ONs powder was also tested by a time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer, and the fluorescence attenuation curve of the material was obtained, as shown in Fig. 4f. The fluorescence lifetime of the polymer coating structured ONs powder is 1.71 ns. It is obviously shorter than that of ONs dispersed in aqueous solution, revealing that there occurred resonance energy transfer in the polymer coating structured ONs powder.

To study the formation of polymer coating ONs structures and explore the effect of Zn2+ on the formation of polymer-like coated structures, samples without Zn2+ were prepared at the same conditions. The TEM image reveals that the structure of the prepared ultrasmall ONs without Zn2+ are not covered by polymer coating, as shown in Fig. S1. The comparison of XRD diffraction patterns shows that fabricated ONs without Zn2+ (Fig. S2) don’t display the peak at the position of ∼ 11° and just show a broad peak at the position of ∼ 22°, indicating the absence of the structure of silicone network. In addition, the fluorescence spectra of ONs synthesized without Zn2+ are shown in Fig. S3. This reveals that the fluorescence properties of the synthesized ONs without Zn2+ addition are almost the same with those of the polymer coating structured ONs in aqueous solutions. Thus, Zn2+ ions have an influence on the formation of polymer structures but have little effect on the ONs formation and their emission properties. In order to further study the influence of Zn2+ ions on the ONs structure, we compared the FTIR spectra of samples prepared with and without Zn2+, as shown in Fig. S4. Results reveal that there is an increasing of the vibration of SiOSi at the position of 1090 cm−1, while the vibration of SiOH at the position of 918 cm−1 is reduced when Zn2+ is added. This shows that Zn2+ promotes the SiOH reaction to form a structure of SiOSi ring/network, corresponding to the polymer coating structure. Except for the two absorption peaks, the other peaks are almost the same in the samples. The XRD and FT-IR results show that the addition of Zn2+ ions improves SiOSi ring/network structure formation, leading to form polymer coating structures. The structure can prevent the destruction of fluorescent groups during aggregation so that the polymer coating structured ONs are able to still maintain good fluorescence performance in the aggregation state.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of ONs powder in fingerprint detection
ce:section.ce:para: Trace fingerprints are considered as the very important evidence in the judicial systems. Potential fingerprints cannot be visible under normal circumstances which need to be identified through special approaches. Nowadays, commonly used fingerprint methods have been gradually developed, including the cyanoacrylate fuming, small particle reagent, and the powder detection technology. Recently, fluorescent nanomaterials have been employed in fingerprint identification to improve the optical contrast between fingerprints and substrates. Based on this, inorganic nanoparticles including gold nanoparticles and semiconductor quantum dots have been used for potential fingerprint imaging. Unfortunately, the complex preparation process and relatively high toxicity of inorganic nanoparticles significantly hinder their application in fingerprint detection. Thus, developing low toxic fluorescent materials is critical to realize clear fingerprint visualization.

Polymer coating structured ONs are rich in amino hydroxyl groups and can interact with oils or proteins, thus ONs can be applied to fingerprint detection to realize potential fingerprint detection simply and quickly. By coating the prepared polymer coated dye ONs phosphor on aluminum foil printed with fingerprints, three layers of fingerprints, whorl, delta, bifurcation, termination, scar and island, are able to be clearly detected under irradiation with an ultraviolet lamp, as shown in Fig. 5 .

The prepared ONs powder can also be applied to different substrates to provide high-resolution fingerprint detection. As shown in Fig. 6 , the details of fingerprints, including whorl and termination, are able to be clearly detected by coating the orange ONs powder on the surfaces of glass, plastic, weighing paper and coins. On these different substrate surfaces, the fluorescence of the powder had no change, which reveals that the fluorescence properties of the powder were relatively stable and were unaffected by various surface chemical properties. Therefore, the ONs phosphor is able to achieve fingerprint recognition on various substrates without post-treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of ONs powder in WLED devices
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the excellent solid-state fluorescence characteristics of ONs, they are able to be applied to WLED devices. The prepared fluorescent powder was mixed with commercial green silicate powder (Ba, Sr)2SiO4:Eu2+ and deposited on the blue chip at 460 nm by combining with epoxy resin to prepare WLEDs. Fig. S5a displays the photo of the WLED and the PL emission spectrum under 20 mA driving current. The color coordinates of the LED are located at (0.3447, 0.3290), which is close to the color coordinates of pure white light (0.33, 0.33), as shown in Fig. S5b. The color rendering index (CRI) of WLEDs is 60.9, and the color temperature (CCT) is 4926 K. These results prove that the synthesized ONs powder is able to be a substitute for commercial fluorescent powder to fabricate WLED device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ONs serve as a new LysoTracker
ce:section.ce:para: The ONs in aqueous solutions exhibited a strong emission at 525 nm under excitation wavelengths from 435 nm to 525 nm, indicating the potential of serving as a nanoprobe for cellular imaging. As shown in Fig. S6, ONs could be effectively internalized into human hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) BEL 7402 cells within 1 h post-incubation. Intriguingly, the signals derived from the ONs (green fluorescence) overlapped perfectly with that of commercially available LysoTracker Red DND-99 (red fluorescence), producing significant yellow regions (Fig. S6). Moreover, colocalization was further evidenced by a close synchrony in the fluorescence intensity profiles, suggesting the specific accumulation of ONs in endo/lysosomes. Noticeably, ONs exhibited a long-term endo-lysosomes tracking performance, with no ONs translocated to other organelles following 4 h incubation. Such specific staining of endo/lysosome compartments was further verified in two additional tested cell lines, namely HCC SUN-449 cells and Raw 264.7 macrophages (Fig. 7 a). Obviously, the excellent colocalization of ONs with LysoTracker Red DND-99 was observed in all tested live cells, demonstrating that ONs is capable of staining endo/lysosomes. Furthermore, to examine whether ONs can stain endo/lysosomes with high selectivity, we contained SUN-449 cells with other commercial organelle tracker probes, including MitoTracker Red CMXRos for mitochondria, ER-Tracker Red for the endoplasmic reticulum and Hoechst 33324 for the cellular nucleus. The CLSM results showed ONs failed to label mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum and nucleus, and the fluorescence signal of ONs was completely distinguished from that of commercial organelle trackers (Fig. 7b). Hence, we concluded that ONs could be applied as a novel endo/lysosome tracker for living cells.

Compared to the effective internalization of ONs at 37 °C, the cellular uptake was remarkably impaired by lowering the temperature of cell culture to 4 °C, which was consistent with the energy-dependent endocytosis mechanism (Fig. 7c). The detailed endocytic pathway of ONs was determined by incubation with distinct inhibitors. As shown in Fig. 7d, treatment with chlorpromazine (an inhibitor of clathrin-dependent endocytosis) resulted in a remarkable reduction in ONs uptake to 87.9%. Intriguingly, the internalization of ONs also decreased in the presence of cytochalasin D (an inhibitor of pinocytosis-mediated endocytosis, P < 0.05). However, filipin III, a caveolin-mediated endocytosis inhibitor, had no effect on the cellular uptake of ONs. These results indicated that the internalization of ONs was predominated by clathrin-dependent endocytosis; however, pinocytosis was also involved in the ONs uptake.

We further examined the biocompatibility of ONs, which is essential for future cell imaging. Accordingly, the cytotoxicity of ONs across distinct cell lines was determined via the CCK-8 assay. Notably, cells treated with ONs over a concentration range from 0.028 to 28.0 µg/mL exhibited comparably high viabilities relative to untreated cells, indicating the low cytotoxicity of ONs (Fig. S7).

Commercial lysosomal tracker probes, including LysoTracker Green DND-26 and LysoTracker Red DND-99, are dispersed in endo/lysosomes and are susceptible to photobleaching after continuous laser irradiation. Thus, photostability is regarded as an important parameter for long-term observations under irradiation. Here, the photostability of ONs was compared with that of LysoTracker Red DND-99. For this purpose, endo/lysosomes were costained with ONs and LysoTracker Red DND-99, and then observed on CLSM. As shown in Fig. 8 a, following 30 scans (approximately 8 min of laser irradiation), the fluorescence intensity of LysoTracker Red DND-99 was reduced by 58.6% relative to its initial intensity. Encouragingly, no photobleaching was observed in the fluorescence signals when ONs were used as a lysotracker, demonstrating their excellent photostability (Fig. 8b). The favorable photostability might be attributable to the intrinsic nanostructures that protected the embedded fluorescence dyes from photodecomposition.

In this work, polymer coating structured ultrasmall ONs with strong solid-state orange fluorescence were designed and synthesized through a simple one-step hydrothermal approach. The resultant ultrasmall ONs were successfully employed to achieve fingerprint recognition on a variety of substrates with high contrast. Furthermore, due to excellent solid-state fluorescence, ultrasmall ONs powder are able to be used to fabricate WLEDs when mixed with commercial green fluorescent powders on 460 nm chips. Interestingly, upon addition to living cells, these water-soluble ONs can specifically target and label lysosomes rather than other organelles. Of particular note, these water-soluble ONs are remarkably photostable under laser irradiation over commercially available expensive LysoTrackers and show low cytotoxicity, rendering their potential use for the long-term study of lysosome-associated diseases. Our results have demonstrated that ultrasmall ONs show great potential in many areas, such as biology and photoelectricity.
All data are available in the manuscript or the supplementary materials.

ce:para.ce:italic: All experiments in this article do not include human or animal related research.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Research Project of Jinan Microecological Biomedicine Shandong Laboratory (JNL-2022010B), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 82073296, 81773193, and 21774098), and Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LR19H160002). This study is also financially supported by the Opening Project of State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering of China (Sichuan University) (Grant No. sklpme2019-4-26).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jare.2022.07.006.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
